items.question_text: Use TBL 14-2-2 and TBL 14-2-3. In domestic en route CPDLC, which table family should you use to distinguish controller-issued route clearances from pilot route requests or confirmations?
items.explanation: TBL 14-2-2 contains Route Uplink Message Elements, while TBL 14-2-3 contains Route Downlink Message Elements. The direction of the CPDLC message determines which source table applies.

choices.text: Route uplink for controller-issued route messages; route downlink for pilot route requests or confirmations.
Standard response uplink for route clearances; free text downlink for route confirmations.
Emergency uplink for all route changes.
System management downlink for route clearances.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-2-4 and TBL 14-2-5. A CPDLC message concerns lateral navigation rather than altitude or speed. What is the first source-use decision?
items.explanation: TBL 14-2-4 contains Lateral Uplink Message Elements and TBL 14-2-5 contains Lateral Downlink Message Elements. Decide whether ATC is sending an instruction or the aircraft is sending a request/report.

choices.text: Determine whether the lateral message is an uplink from ATC or a downlink from the aircraft.
Treat every lateral message as a voice communications message.
Use only the speed table because lateral messages imply speed control.
Use emergency/urgency tables for all lateral messages.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-2-7, TBL 14-2-8, TBL 14-2-9, and TBL 14-2-10. Which pairing correctly maps domestic CPDLC message subject to table family?
items.explanation: TBL 14-2-7 is Altitude Downlink, TBL 14-2-8 is Crossing Constraint Uplink, TBL 14-2-9 is Speed Uplink, and TBL 14-2-10 is Speed Downlink.

choices.text: Altitude downlink, crossing-constraint uplink, speed uplink, and speed downlink are separate table families.
All altitude, crossing, and speed messages are route uplinks.
Crossing constraints are only emergency downlinks.
Speed uplinks and speed downlinks use the same table regardless of message direction.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-2-11, TBL 14-2-12, TBL 14-2-13, TBL 14-2-14, and TBL 14-2-15. Which source-use rule prevents mixing advisory, voice-communications, and emergency/urgency CPDLC messages?
items.explanation: TBL 14-2-11 covers Air Traffic Advisory Uplink, TBL 14-2-12 and TBL 14-2-13 cover Voice Communications Uplink/Downlink, and TBL 14-2-14 and TBL 14-2-15 cover Emergency/Urgency Uplink/Downlink.

choices.text: Select the table by message purpose and direction: advisory, voice communications, or emergency/urgency; uplink versus downlink still matters.
Use advisory uplinks for all voice and emergency messages.
Use emergency downlink tables whenever ATC sends a message.
Ignore direction because all CPDLC message elements have the same intended use.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-2-16, TBL 14-2-17, TBL 14-2-18, and TBL 14-2-19. Which domestic CPDLC table split keeps response-message elements separate from supplemental-message elements?
items.explanation: TBL 14-2-16 and TBL 14-2-17 are Standard Response Uplink/Downlink tables. TBL 14-2-18 and TBL 14-2-19 are Supplemental Uplink/Downlink tables.

choices.text: Standard response and supplemental messages each have separate uplink and downlink tables.
Supplemental messages are always entered through standard response downlinks.
Standard response messages are only route uplinks.
Downlink response messages are stored in the voice communications table.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-2-20, TBL 14-2-21, TBL 14-2-22, and TBL 14-2-23. For domestic CPDLC, what distinction separates free-text tables from system-management tables?
items.explanation: TBL 14-2-20 and TBL 14-2-21 contain Free Text Uplink/Downlink elements. TBL 14-2-22 and TBL 14-2-23 contain System Management Uplink/Downlink elements.

choices.text: Free-text elements carry text message content; system-management elements manage the data-link service or connection context.
Free-text tables are only for emergency messages.
System-management tables are only for speed assignments.
Free-text and system-management tables are interchangeable.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-3-2 and TBL 14-3-3. In oceanic ATOP CPDLC abnormal-condition context, which table family separates route uplinks from route downlinks?
items.explanation: TBL 14-3-2 contains Route Uplink Message Elements and TBL 14-3-3 contains Route Downlink Message Elements. Message direction remains the first sorting decision.

choices.text: TBL 14-3-2 for route uplinks and TBL 14-3-3 for route downlinks.
TBL 14-3-10 for route uplinks and TBL 14-3-24 for route downlinks.
Emergency/urgency tables for every route message.
System-management tables for every route clearance.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-3-4 and TBL 14-3-5. An oceanic CPDLC message concerns lateral navigation. What determines which lateral table applies?
items.explanation: TBL 14-3-4 is Lateral Uplink Message Elements, while TBL 14-3-5 is Lateral Downlink Message Elements.

choices.text: Whether ATC sends the lateral message as an uplink or the aircraft sends it as a downlink.
Whether the aircraft is above FL390.
Whether the message mentions weather abbreviations.
Whether the aircraft is using voice only.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-3-6, TBL 14-3-7, TBL 14-3-8, TBL 14-3-9, and TBL 14-3-10. Which grouping correctly describes the oceanic altitude, crossing, and speed message tables?
items.explanation: TBL 14-3-6 and TBL 14-3-7 are Altitude Uplink/Downlink, TBL 14-3-8 is Crossing Constraint Uplink, and TBL 14-3-9 and TBL 14-3-10 are Speed Uplink/Downlink.

choices.text: Altitude has uplink/downlink tables, crossing constraints have an uplink table, and speed has uplink/downlink tables.
Altitude, crossing, and speed are all handled only as route downlinks.
Crossing constraints are system-management messages.
Speed messages are always emergency/urgency messages.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-3-11, TBL 14-3-12, TBL 14-3-13, TBL 14-3-14, TBL 14-3-15, and TBL 14-3-16. Which source-use distinction keeps advisory, voice-communications, and emergency/urgency messages from being conflated?
items.explanation: TBL 14-3-11 and TBL 14-3-12 cover Air Traffic Advisory Uplink/Downlink, TBL 14-3-13 and TBL 14-3-14 cover Voice Communications Uplink/Downlink, and TBL 14-3-15 and TBL 14-3-16 cover Emergency/Urgency Uplink/Downlink.

choices.text: Use both message purpose and direction: advisory, voice, and emergency/urgency each have their own uplink/downlink source tables.
All advisory, voice, and emergency messages use the route uplink table.
Only emergency/urgency messages have a downlink form.
Voice communications tables are used only for speed restrictions.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-3-17, TBL 14-3-18, TBL 14-3-19, and TBL 14-3-20. In oceanic CPDLC, how are standard response messages separated from supplemental messages?
items.explanation: TBL 14-3-17 and TBL 14-3-18 are Standard Response Uplink/Downlink. TBL 14-3-19 and TBL 14-3-20 are Supplemental Uplink/Downlink.

choices.text: Standard response and supplemental messages have distinct uplink and downlink table families.
Supplemental messages are always emergency downlinks.
Standard response messages are only advisory uplinks.
Both families are covered only by the route downlink table.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-3-21, TBL 14-3-22, TBL 14-3-23, and TBL 14-3-24. What is the difference between free-text and system-management CPDLC table families in oceanic operations?
items.explanation: TBL 14-3-21 and TBL 14-3-22 contain Free Text Uplink/Downlink elements, while TBL 14-3-23 and TBL 14-3-24 contain System Management Uplink/Downlink elements.

choices.text: Free-text tables contain text-message elements; system-management tables contain service/connection-management elements.
Free-text tables are used only for altitude clearances.
System-management tables are used only for aircraft speed.
The two families are interchangeable because both are downlinks.

items.question_text: Use TBL 14-3-26. What role does the additional downlink message table serve relative to the named route, lateral, altitude, speed, advisory, voice, emergency, response, supplemental, free-text, and system-management tables?
items.explanation: TBL 14-3-26 is the Additional Downlink Messages table. It is a residual source for downlink message elements that are not handled by the more specific named downlink families.

choices.text: It captures additional downlink message elements outside the more specific named downlink families.
It replaces all uplink message-element tables.
It is used only for runway separation.
It is the domestic route-uplink table.
